title: Listening - Part 1
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1–6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear. There are two questions for each extract.

extracts.context: You hear two friends discussing a new city-wide bike-sharing scheme.
extracts.audio_script: Man: I’ve finally signed up for 'City-Cycles'. It’s much cheaper than the bus for my commute. || Woman: [1]I found the app's GPS to be quite unreliable at times[/1], to be honest. It said there were five bikes available at the station near my office, but when I got there, it was empty. || Man: I had the same issue! It’s a bit of a gamble. || Woman: [2]What I really appreciated, though, was the build quality of the bikes[/2]. They feel much more robust than those flimsy ones they had in the previous scheme. || Man: I agree. They handle the potholes on the high street quite well. || Woman: Exactly. It’s a step in the right direction, provided they can fix the technical glitches with the docking stations.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I’ve finally signed up for 'City-Cycles'. It’s much cheaper than the bus for my commute.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I found the app's GPS to be quite unreliable at times, to be honest. It said there were five bikes available at the station near my office, but when I got there, it was empty.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I had the same issue! It’s a bit of a gamble.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: What I really appreciated, though, was the build quality of the bikes. They feel much more robust than those flimsy ones they had in the previous scheme.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I agree. They handle the potholes on the high street quite well.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Exactly. It’s a step in the right direction, provided they can fix the technical glitches with the docking stations.

questions.question: What is the woman's main complaint about the bike-sharing scheme?

options.A: the high cost of the monthly subscription
options.B: the inaccuracy of the real-time information
options.C: the poor maintenance of the bicycles

questions.explanation: She mentions the app's GPS is 'unreliable' and gave incorrect information about bike availability [1].

questions.question: Both speakers are impressed by the bicycles'
questions.explanation: They both praise the 'build quality' and that they feel 'robust' compared to previous models [2].

extracts.context: You hear two colleagues talking about a new productivity technique called 'time-blocking'.
extracts.audio_script: Man: I’ve started using 'time-blocking' for my daily tasks. It’s really helped me focus on my deep work. || Woman: [3]I suspect it only works if you have total control over your schedule[/3], which is impossible in this office with all the 'urgent' requests. || Man: It is a challenge. I’ve had to start ignoring my emails for two hours a day. || Woman: [4]I’ve noticed that it can make you quite rigid and less willing to help colleagues[/4] because you’re so focused on your 'block'. || Man: I agree. It can feel a bit antisocial at times. || Woman: Exactly. It’s a good tool for freelancers, perhaps, but it’s a bit hard to implement in a collaborative team environment.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I’ve started using 'time-blocking' for my daily tasks. It’s really helped me focus on my deep work.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I suspect it only works if you have total control over your schedule, which is impossible in this office with all the 'urgent' requests.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: It is a challenge. I’ve had to start ignoring my emails for two hours a day.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I’ve noticed that it can make you quite rigid and less willing to help colleagues because you’re so focused on your 'block'.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I agree. It can feel a bit antisocial at times.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Exactly. It’s a good tool for freelancers, perhaps, but it’s a bit hard to implement in a collaborative team environment.

questions.question: The woman believes the technique is difficult to use in their office because

options.A: of the high volume of unexpected demands.
options.B: the management does not support it.
options.C: the software they use is incompatible.

questions.explanation: She mentions it's impossible due to all the 'urgent' requests they receive [3].

questions.question: What negative social effect of the technique do both speakers identify?

options.A: It increases competition between team members.
options.C: It leads to more frequent workplace conflicts.

questions.explanation: They agree it makes people 'less willing to help colleagues' and feels 'antisocial' [4].

extracts.context: You hear two people discussing a recently published travel book about Japan.
extracts.audio_script: Man: I’ve just finished 'Neon and Zen'. It’s a fascinating look at the contrasts in Japanese culture. || Woman: [5]I found the author's focus on really expensive luxury hotels a bit alienating[/5], to be honest. It’s not exactly a guide for the average traveller. || Man: I see your point. It was a bit 'high-end'. || Woman: [6]I did appreciate the quality of the photography, though[/6]. The shots of the rural shrines were absolutely stunning. || Man: I agree. It’s worth buying just for the visuals alone. || Woman: Definitely. It’s a beautiful coffee-table book, even if the travel advice is a bit out of reach for most of us.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I’ve just finished 'Neon and Zen'. It’s a fascinating look at the contrasts in Japanese culture.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I found the author's focus on really expensive luxury hotels a bit alienating, to be honest. It’s not exactly a guide for the average traveller.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I see what you mean. It was a bit 'high-end'.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I did appreciate the quality of the photography, though. The shots of the rural shrines were absolutely stunning.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I agree. It’s worth buying just for the visuals alone.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Definitely. It’s a beautiful coffee-table book, even if the travel advice is a bit out of reach for most of us.

questions.question: What was the woman's main criticism of the book?

options.B: the elitist nature of the recommendations
options.C: the poor quality of the translation

questions.explanation: She found the focus on 'luxury hotels' to be 'alienating' and 'high-end' [5].

questions.question: Both speakers were impressed by the book's
questions.explanation: They both praise the 'quality of the photography' and the 'visuals' [6].
